The bulk of the training focuses on recovering small-scale structures, while fine-tuning in high-noise regimes enables the generation of extremes. Evaluation against the medium-range IFS ensemble target shows that the model increases probabilistic skill (FCRPS) for surface variables, reproduces target power spectra at small scales, captures physically consistent multivariate relationships such as wind-pressure coupling, and generates extreme values consistent with those of the target ensemble in tropical cyclones.

</details>


### [714] [Physics-Constrained Adaptive Flow Matching for Climate Downscaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03459)
*Kevin Debeire,Aytaç Paçal,Pierre Gentine,Luis Medrano-Navarro,Nils Thuerey,Veronika Eyring*

Main category: physics.ao-ph

TL;DR: The abstract introduces PC-AFM, a generative climate downscaling model that incorporates physical constraints to enhance accuracy, consistency, and generalization beyond the training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regional climate projections at high resolution are critical for climate impact assessments, yet computationally prohibitive using global models. Machine learning offers speed but struggles with physical law violations and generalization beyond its training distribution.

Method: PC-AFM builds on Adaptive Flow Matching, integrating soft conservation constraints and gradient surgery via ConFIG to ensure large-scale input-output consistency in downscaled results. It was trained on Central Europe's climate data, evaluated across metrics, and tested on unseen regions.

Result: PC-AFM demonstrated reduced conservation errors, improved ensemble calibration, and maintained skill metrics within the training scope. Outside the training distribution, it halved precipitation bias, reduced errors, and enhanced extreme-quantile accuracy without extra climate-specific data.

Conclusion: By addressing physical consistency and generalization issues, PC-AFM showcases the potential of physically-constrained machine learning models for accurate, scalable climate downscaling in real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Regional climate information at kilometer scales is essential for assessing the impacts of climate change, but generating it with global climate models is too expensive due to their high computational costs. Machine learning models offer a fast alternative, yet they often violate basic physical laws and degrade when applied to climates outside of their training distribution. We present Physics-Constrained Adaptive Flow Matching (PC-AFM), a generative downscaling model that addresses both problems. Building on the Adaptive Flow Matching (AFM) model of Fotiadis et al. (2025) as our baseline, we add soft conservation constraints that keep the downscaled output consistent with the large-scale input for precipitation and humidity, and use gradient surgery via the ConFIG algorithm to prevent these constraints from interfering with the generative objective. We train the model on Central Europe climate data, evaluate it on a 10-time downscaling task (63km to 6.3km) over six variables (near-surface temperature, precipitation, specific humidity, surface pressure, and horizontal wind components) across a comprehensive set of metrics including bias, ensemble skill scores, power spectra, and conservation error, and test the generalization on two held-out climate regions. Within the training distribution, PC-AFM reduces conservation errors and improves ensemble calibration while matching the baseline on standard skill metrics. Outside the training distribution, where unconstrained models develop large systematic errors by extrapolating learned statistics, PC-AFM halves precipitation wet bias, reduces conservation error and improves extreme-quantile accuracy, all without any information about the target climate at inference time. These results indicate that physical consistency is a practical requirement for deploying generative downscaling models in real-world applications.

</details>


<div id='cs.SI'></div>

# cs.SI [[Back]](#toc)

### [715] [Transmission Neural Networks: Inhibitory and Excitatory Connections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04246)
*Shuang Gao,Peter E. Caines*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: This paper enhances the Transmission Neural Network model by integrating inhibitory connections and neurotransmitter populations, characterizing firing probabilities, and exploring stability and contraction under limiting conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to extend the original Transmission Neural Network model to account for more biologically accurate dynamics, such as inhibitory connections and neurotransmitter populations, to provide a deeper understanding of neuronal behavior.

Method: The study develops a mathematical framework incorporating binary neuronal states, inhibitory/excitatory connections, and neurotransmitter dynamics, analyzing behavior under limiting conditions and establishing conditions for stability.

Result: Characterization of neuron firing probabilities is achieved, including an equivalence representation using a 2-dimensional continuous neuron state. The limit network model with infinite neurotransmitters is explored, and stability conditions are derived.

Conclusion: The extended neural network model provides a more accurate and theoretically grounded representation of neuronal dynamics, including inhibitory effects and neurotransmitter influence, with stability conditions established.

Abstract: This paper extends the Transmission Neural Network model proposed by Gao and Caines in [1]-[3] to incorporate inhibitory connections and neurotransmitter populations. The extended network model contains binary neuronal states, transmission dynamics, and inhibitory and excitatory connections. Under technical assumptions, we establish the characterization of the firing probabilities of neurons, and show that such a characterization considering inhibitions can be equivalently represented by a neural network where each neuron has a continuous state of dimension 2. Moreover, we incorporated neurotransmitter populations into the modeling and establish the limit network model when the number of neurotransmitters at all synaptic connections go to infinity. Finally, sufficient conditions for stability and contraction properties of the limit network model are established.

</details>


<div id='physics.acc-ph'></div>

# physics.acc-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [716] [PhaseFlow4D: Physically Constrained 4D Beam Reconstruction via Feedback-Guided Latent Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03885)
*Alexander Scheinker,Alexander Plastun,Peter Ostroumov*

Main category: physics.acc-ph

TL;DR: This paper introduces PhaseFlow4D, a model to reconstruct 4D transverse phase space distributions from sparse 2D projections, achieving physical consistency and high speed compared to traditional simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to recover high-dimensional distributions (specifically 4D transverse phase space densities) in particle accelerators is critical, yet direct measurement is physically impossible.

Method: The method leverages a 4D Variational Autoencoder (VAE) with a hard projection-consistency constraint, combined with an adaptive feedback loop in a latent diffusion model for real-time distribution tracking.

Result: PhaseFlow4D can reconstruct 4D phase space distributions 11,000 times faster than traditional methods while maintaining accuracy, as demonstrated in heavy-ion beam simulations.

Conclusion: The proposed approach showcases a robust and efficient generative reconstruction method for high-dimensional, time-varying distributions, with potential applications beyond the visual domain.

Abstract: We address the problem of recovering a time-varying 4D distribution from a sparse sequence of 2D projections - analogous to novel-view synthesis from sparse cameras, but applied to the 4D transverse phase space density $ρ(x,p_x,y,p_y)$ of charged particle beams. Direct single shot measurement of this high-dimensional distribution is physically impossible in real particle accelerator systems; only limited 1D or 2D projections are accessible. We propose PhaseFlow4D, a feedback-guided latent diffusion model that reconstructs and tracks the full 4D phase space from incomplete 2D observations alone, with built-in hard physics constraints. Our core technical contribution is a 4D VAE whose decoder generates the full 4D phase space tensor, from which 2D projections are analytically computed and compared against 2D beam measurements. This projection-consistency constraint guarantees physical correctness by construction - not as a soft penalty, but as an architectural prior. An adaptive feedback loop then continuously tunes the conditioning vector of the latent diffusion model to track time-varying distributions online without retraining. We validate on multi-particle simulations of heavy-ion beams at the Facility for Rare Isotope Beams (FRIB), where full physics simulations require $\sim$6 hours on a 100-core HPC system. PhaseFlow4D achieves accurate 4D reconstructions 11000$\times$ faster while faithfully tracking distribution shifts under time-varying source conditions - demonstrating that principled generative reconstruction under incomplete observations transfers robustly beyond visual domains.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.str-el'></div>

# cond-mat.str-el [[Back]](#toc)

### [717] [Expressibility of neural quantum states: a Walsh-complexity perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03294)
*Taige Wang*

Main category: cond-mat.str-el

TL;DR: The paper introduces Walsh complexity, a measure of wavefunction spread over parity patterns, showing its implications for shallow additive feed-forward networks' expressibility in representing quantum states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental limits and expressibility of modern additive neural quantum states in efficiently representing many-body quantum states.

Method: The authors define Walsh complexity as a basis-dependent measure and analyze the ability of neural quantum states to represent states with varying complexity, examining depth and activation scaling effects.

Result: Shallow feed-forward networks struggle to produce high Walsh complexity without logarithmic depth scaling or certain activations. A dimerized state with maximal Walsh complexity challenges these architectures.

Conclusion: Depth is an essential resource for additive neural quantum states to capture high-complexity states, with Walsh complexity offering a complementary expressibility perspective to entanglement metrics.

Abstract: Neural quantum states are powerful variational wavefunctions, but it remains unclear which many-body states can be represented efficiently by modern additive architectures. We introduce Walsh complexity, a basis-dependent measure of how broadly a wavefunction is spread over parity patterns. States with an almost uniform Walsh spectrum require exponentially large Walsh complexity from any good approximant. We show that shallow additive feed-forward networks cannot generate such complexity in the tame regime, e.g. polynomial activations with subexponential parameter scaling. As a concrete example, we construct a simple dimerized state prepared by a single layer of disjoint controlled-$Z$ gates. Although it has only short-range entanglement and a simple tensor-network description, its Walsh complexity is maximal. Full-cube fits across system size and depth are consistent with the complexity bound: for polynomial activations, successful fitting appears only once depth reaches a logarithmic scale in $N$, whereas activation saturation in $\tanh$ produces a sharp threshold-like jump already at depth $3$. Walsh complexity therefore provides an expressibility axis complementary to entanglement and clarifies when depth becomes an essential resource for additive neural quantum states.

</details>


<div id='math.DG'></div>

# math.DG [[Back]](#toc)

### [718] [Minimising Willmore Energy via Neural Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04321)
*Edward Hirst,Henrique N. Sá Earp,Tomás S. R. Silva*

Main category: math.DG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a neural approach to minimize Willmore energy for 2-surfaces using PINN-styled learning, successfully reproducing known minimal surfaces for genus 0 and 1 and exploring solutions for genus 2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The goal is to use neural architectures to minimize the Willmore energy of closed 2-surfaces, addressing an open mathematical problem of finding minimal Willmore surfaces.

Method: Neural networks are trained with PINN-style loss to model embeddings from 2D to 3D while minimizing Willmore energy. Experiments involve training for different genus surfaces.

Result: The trained models successfully reproduce known minimal Willmore surfaces, like the round sphere (genus 0) and the Clifford torus (genus 1). Results for genus 2 provide insights into minimal Willmore solutions.

Conclusion: Using neural networks to minimize Willmore energy is effective for established cases and offers promising directions for unresolved problems, such as genus 2 minimal Willmore surfaces.

Abstract: The neural Willmore flow of a closed oriented $2$-surface in $\mathbb{R}^3$ is introduced as a natural evolution process to minimise the Willmore energy, which is the squared $L^2$-norm of mean curvature. Neural architectures are used to model maps from topological $2d$ domains to $3d$ Euclidean space, where the learning process minimises a PINN-style loss for the Willmore energy as a functional on the embedding. Training reproduces the expected round sphere for genus $0$ surfaces, and the Clifford torus for genus $1$ surfaces, respectively. Furthermore, the experiment in the genus $2$ case provides a novel approach to search for minimal Willmore surfaces in this open problem.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [719] [NetSecBed: A Container-Native Testbed for Reproducible Cybersecurity Experimentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04121)
*Leonardo Bitzki,Diego Kreutz,Tiago Heinrich,Douglas Fideles,Leandro Bertholdo,Silvio Quincozes,Angelo Diniz*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces NetSecBed, a framework for generating reproducible cybersecurity datasets utilizing container-native and scenario-oriented methods, especially for multi-protocol IoT and IIoT environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most public cybersecurity datasets are static and lack the flexibility for reproducible testing, especially in heterogeneous environments like IoT and IIoT. The aim is to create a framework to address this reproducibility gap.

Method: The authors developed NetSecBed, a container-based framework that integrates attack scenarios, services, and traffic generators. It operates with declarative specifications for automation and extensibility, covering end-to-end processes like data capturing and feature extraction.

Result: NetSecBed successfully provides repeatable experiments with 60 attack scenarios, various traffic types, and enables easy dataset creation, enhancing traceability and minimizing operational biases.

Conclusion: The paper offers a reproducible, scalable solution for cybersecurity research, addressing key limitations in traditional datasets and fostering advanced experimentation in diverse environments.

Abstract: Cybersecurity research increasingly depends on reproducible evidence, such as traffic traces, logs, and labeled datasets, yet most public datasets remain static and offer limited support for controlled re-execution and traceability, especially in heterogeneous multi-protocol environments. This paper presents NetSecBed, a container-native, scenario-oriented testbed for reproducible generation of network traffic evidence and execution artifacts under controlled conditions, particularly suitable for IoT, IIoT, and pervasive multi-protocol environments. The framework integrates 60 attack scenarios, 9 target services, and benign traffic generators as single-purpose containers, enabling plug-and-play extensibility and traceability through declarative specifications. Its pipeline automates parametrized execution, packet capture, log collection, service probing, feature extraction, and dataset consolidation. The main contribution is a repeatable, auditable, and extensible framework for cybersecurity experimentation that reduces operational bias and supports continuous dataset generation.

</details>


### [720] [AEGIS: Scaling Long-Sequence Homomorphic Encrypted Transformer Inference via Hybrid Parallelism on Multi-GPU Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03425)
*Zhaoting Gong,Ran Ran,Fan Yao,Wujie Wen*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: AEGIS presents a scalable method for long-sequence encrypted Transformer inference on multi-GPU platforms, addressing memory and communication bottlenecks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenges of memory capacity and inter-GPU communication in multi-GPU platforms during encrypted Transformer inference, especially for long-sequence inputs.

Method: AEGIS introduces an Application-Encryption Guided Inference System that smartly organizes device placement and reorders operations to optimize inter-GPU communication and exploit computation overlap.

Result: AEGIS reduces inter-GPU communication significantly (up to 81.3% in self-attention), achieves a scaling efficiency of up to 96.62%, improves end-to-end speedup by 3.86x on four GPUs, and reduces per-device memory usage by 69.1%.

Conclusion: AEGIS demonstrates that its coordinated approach to application-encryption parallelism overcomes core limitations, making scalable homomorphic Transformer inference feasible and efficient on multi-GPU systems.

Abstract: Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) enables privacy-preserving Transformer inference, but long-sequence encrypted Transformers quickly exceed single-GPU memory capacity because encoded weights are already large and encrypted activations grow rapidly with sequence length. Multi-GPU execution therefore becomes unavoidable, yet scaling remains challenging because communication is jointly induced by application-level aggregation and encryption-level RNS coupling. Existing approaches either synchronize between devices frequently or replicate encrypted tensors across devices, leading to excessive communication and latency.
  We present AEGIS, an Application-Encryption Guided Inference System for scalable long-sequence encrypted Transformer inference on multi-GPU platforms. AEGIS derives device placement from ciphertext dependencies jointly induced by Transformer dataflow and CKKS polynomial coupling, co-locating modulus-coherent and token-coherent data so that communication is introduced only when application dependencies require it, while reordering polynomial operators to overlap the remaining collectives with computation.
  On 2048-token inputs, AEGIS reduces inter-GPU communication by up to 57.9% in feed-forward networks and 81.3% in self-attention versus prior state-of-the-art designs. On four GPUs, it achieves up to 96.62% scaling efficiency, 3.86x end-to-end speedup, and 69.1% per-device memory reduction. These results establish coordinated application-encryption parallelism as a practical foundation for scalable homomorphic Transformer inference.

</details>


### [721] [Enabling Deterministic User-Level Interrupts in Real-Time Processors via Hardware Extension](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04015)
*Hongbin Yang,Huanle Zhang,Runyu Pan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel hardware extension that enables deterministic, efficient interrupt handling for real-time embedded systems, reducing worst-case latency by over 50x.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current embedded system designs struggle with balancing interrupt latency reduction and maintaining stringent isolation for untrusted software components, impacting critical applications such as autonomous vehicles.

Method: The authors propose a hardware extension for deterministic switching to proper protection domains upon interrupt arrival, bypassing kernel intervention for improved efficiency.

Result: The proposed hardware extension reduces worst-case interrupt latency by more than 50x, with modest increases in core area (19%) and dynamic power (4.1%).

Conclusion: This is the first mechanism integrating nanosecond-scale interrupt delivery while ensuring bounded latency without kernel reliance for real-time performance enhancement.

Abstract: The growing complexity of real-time embedded systems demands strong isolation of software components into separate protection domains to reduce attack surfaces and limit fault propagation. However, application-supplied device interrupt handlers -- even untrusted -- have to remain in the kernel to minimize interrupt latency, undermining security and burdening manual certifications. Current hardware extensions accelerate interrupts only when the target protection domain is scheduled by the kernel; consequently, they are limited to improving average-case performance but not worst-case latency, and do not meet the requirements of critical real-time applications such as autonomous vehicles or robots.
  To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel hardware extension that enables direct, deterministic switching to the appropriate protection domain upon user-level interrupt arrival -- without kernel intervention -- even when that domain is dormant. Our hardware extension reduces worst-case latency by more than 50x with a 19% increase in core area (2% of total die area) and 4.1% increase in dynamic power. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first integrated mechanism to guarantee user-level interrupt delivery with a nanosecond-scale yet bounded worst-case latency.

</details>


### [722] [GPIR: Enabling Practical Private Information Retrieval with GPUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04696)
*Hyesung Ji,Hyunah Yu,Jongmin Kim,Wonseok Choi,G. Edward Suh,Jung Ho Ahn*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: GPIR, a GPU-optimized PIR system, redefines kernel design and execution strategies to massively improve throughput, addressing bottlenecks in GPU processing for PIR tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GPU-based PIR systems face inefficiencies due to performance mismatches, particularly in handling multi-client batching and NTT-driven layouts.

Method: The authors designed GPIR with a hybrid execution model, enhanced data layouts, specialized kernel fusion strategies, and multi-GPU scaling.

Result: GPIR achieved up to 305.7x higher throughput than the PIRonGPU system, demonstrating significant optimization.

Conclusion: With dynamic execution models and tailored memory strategies, GPIR establishes itself as a highly efficient framework for GPU-accelerated PIR tasks.

Abstract: Private information retrieval (PIR) allows private database queries but is hindered by intense server-side computation and memory traffic. Modern lattice-based PIR protocols typically involve three phases: ExpandQuery (expanding a query into encrypted indices), RowSel (encrypted row selection), and ColTor (recursive "column tournament" for final selection). ExpandQuery and ColTor primarily perform number-theoretic transforms (NTTs), whereas RowSel reduces to large-scale independent matrix-matrix multiplications (GEMMs). GPUs are theoretically ideal for these tasks, provided multi-client batching is used to achieve high throughput. However, batching fundamentally reshapes performance bottlenecks; while it amortizes database access costs, it expands working sets beyond the L2 cache capacity, causing divergent memory behaviors and excessive DRAM traffic.
  We present GPIR, a GPU-accelerated PIR system that rethinks kernel design, data layout, and execution scheduling. We introduce a stage-aware hybrid execution model that dynamically switches between operation-level kernels, which execute each primitive operation separately, and stage-level kernels, which fuse all operations within a protocol stage into a single kernel to maximize on-chip data reuse. For RowSel, we identify a performance gap caused by a structural mismatch between NTT-driven data layouts and tiled GEMM access patterns, which is exacerbated by multi-client batching. We resolve this through a transposed-layout GEMM design and fine-grained pipelining. Finally, we extend GPIR to multi-GPU systems, scaling both query throughput and database capacity with negligible communication overhead. GPIR achieves up to 305.7x higher throughput than PIRonGPU, the state-of-the-art GPU implementation.

</details>


### [723] [SecureAFL: Secure Asynchronous Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03862)
*Anjun Gao,Feng Wang,Zhenglin Wan,Yueyang Quan,Zhuqing Liu,Minghong Fang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: SecureAFL enhances asynchronous federated learning's robustness against poisoning attacks by detecting anomalous updates and estimating missing client contributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional synchronous FL suffers from the straggler problem, while asynchronous FL's decentralized nature makes it vulnerable to poisoning attacks. Existing defenses neither fully mitigate attacks nor operate under realistic assumptions.

Method: SecureAFL introduces mechanisms to detect and discard anomalous updates, estimate missing client contributions, and incorporates Byzantine-robust aggregation techniques like the coordinate-wise median.

Result: Experiments on various real-world datasets confirm that SecureAFL improves the robustness and effectiveness of asynchronous FL under adversarial conditions.

Conclusion: SecureAFL successfully addresses the shortcomings of existing defenses, providing a practical framework for securing asynchronous FL against poisoning attacks.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables multiple clients to collaboratively train a global machine learning model via a server without sharing their private training data. In traditional FL, the system follows a synchronous approach, where the server waits for model updates from numerous clients before aggregating them to update the global model. However, synchronous FL is hindered by the straggler problem. To address this, the asynchronous FL architecture allows the server to update the global model immediately upon receiving any client's local model update. Despite its advantages, the decentralized nature of asynchronous FL makes it vulnerable to poisoning attacks. Several defenses tailored for asynchronous FL have been proposed, but these mechanisms remain susceptible to advanced attacks or rely on unrealistic server assumptions. In this paper, we introduce SecureAFL, an innovative framework designed to secure asynchronous FL against poisoning attacks. SecureAFL improves the robustness of asynchronous FL by detecting and discarding anomalous updates while estimating the contributions of missing clients. Additionally, it utilizes Byzantine-robust aggregation techniques, such as coordinate-wise median, to integrate the received and estimated updates. Extensive experiments on various real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of SecureAFL.

</details>


### [724] [GPU Acceleration of TFHE-Based High-Precision Nonlinear Layers for Encrypted LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04783)
*Guoci Chen,Xiurui Pan,Qiao Li,Bo Mao,Congming Gao,Chengying Huan,Mingzhe Zhang,Jie Zhang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces TIGER, a GPU-accelerated framework that enhances TFHE-based encrypted inference for LLMs, achieving significant speedups on key nonlinear layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address privacy concerns with LLM inference on sensitive data and overcome the inefficiencies in current FHE methods for high-precision nonlinear function evaluation.

Method: The authors propose GPU-optimized WoP-PBS methods with advanced numerical algorithms, coupled with batch-driven parallelism, to enhance TFHE's precision and efficiency for nonlinear layers.

Result: TIGER achieves substantial speedups—7.17× for GELU, 16.68× for Softmax, and 17.05× for LayerNorm—compared to CPU-based implementations.

Conclusion: TIGER demonstrates the feasibility of high-precision, efficient encrypted inference for nonlinear layers of LLMs, leveraging GPU acceleration to address challenges in TFHE methodologies.

Abstract: Deploying large language models (LLMs) as cloud services raises privacy concerns as inference may leak sensitive data. Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) allows computation on encrypted data, but current FHE methods struggle with efficient and precise nonlinear function evaluation. Specifically, CKKS-based approaches require high-degree polynomial approximations, which are costly when target precision increases. Alternatively, TFHE's Programmable Bootstrapping (PBS) outperforms CKKS by offering exact lookup-table evaluation. But it lacks high-precision implementations of LLM nonlinear layers and underutilizes GPU resources.
  We propose \emph{TIGER}, the first GPU-accelerated framework for high-precision TFHE-based nonlinear LLM layer evaluation. TIGER offers: (1) GPU-optimized WoP-PBS method combined with numerical algorithms to surpass native lookup-table precision limits on nonlinear functions; (2) high-precision and efficient implementations of key nonlinear layers, enabling practical encrypted inference; (3) batch-driven design exploiting inter-input parallelism to boost GPU efficiency. TIGER achieves 7.17$\times$, 16.68$\times$, and 17.05$\times$ speedups over a CPU baseline for GELU, Softmax, and LayerNorm, respectively.

</details>


### [725] [Automating Cloud Security and Forensics Through a Secure-by-Design Generative AI Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03912)
*Dalal Alharthi,Ivan Roberto Kawaminami Garcia*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents a GenAI framework to secure and enhance forensic investigations in cloud environments through PromptShield and CIAF, addressing vulnerabilities of LLMs and ensuring forensic rigor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to tackle the vulnerabilities of LLMs in cybersecurity (e.g., prompt injection attacks) and the need for more effective cloud forensic investigation tools.

Method: Two main components are proposed: PromptShield for securing LLMs via ontology-driven validation, and CIAF for structured, automated cloud forensic analysis across six phases of investigation.

Result: Experimental results reveal that PromptShield achieves high performance under adversarial conditions (93% precision, recall, and F1 scores), and CIAF improves ransomware detection accuracy in cloud logs.

Conclusion: The framework improves security, automation, and reliability in cloud forensic investigations and LLM applications, providing a scalable solution for cloud incident response.

Abstract: As cloud environments become increasingly complex, cybersecurity and forensic investigations must evolve to meet emerging threats. Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise in automating log analysis and reasoning tasks, yet they remain vulnerable to prompt injection attacks and lack forensic rigor. To address these dual challenges, we propose a unified, secure-by-design GenAI framework that integrates PromptShield and the Cloud Investigation Automation Framework (CIAF). PromptShield proactively defends LLMs against adversarial prompts using ontology-driven validation that standardizes user inputs and mitigates manipulation. CIAF streamlines cloud forensic investigations through structured, ontology-based reasoning across all six phases of the forensic process. We evaluate our system on real-world datasets from AWS and Microsoft Azure, demonstrating substantial improvements in both LLM security and forensic accuracy. Experimental results show PromptShield boosts classification performance under attack conditions, achieving precision, recall, and F1 scores above 93%, while CIAF enhances ransomware detection accuracy in cloud logs using Likert-transformed performance features. Our integrated framework advances the automation, interpretability, and trustworthiness of cloud forensics and LLM-based systems, offering a scalable foundation for real-time, AI-driven incident response across diverse cloud infrastructures.

</details>


### [726] [RegGuard: Legitimacy and Fairness Enforcement for Optimistic Rollups](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04748)
*Zhenhang Shang,Yingzhe Yu,Kani Chen*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: RegGuard enhances optimistic rollups for regulated financial use by addressing semantic legitimacy, state consistency, and ordering fairness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimistic rollups are unsuitable for regulated financial applications due to gaps in legitimacy, state consistency, and fairness.

Method: RegGuard uses a semantic validator (RegSpec), a cross-layer state validator, and a cryptographically verifiable fair-ordering service.

Result: Reduced settlement failures by 90%, prevents ordering manipulation, and retains 85% baseline throughput.

Conclusion: RegGuard renders optimistic rollups viable for regulated financial use, bridging critical structural gaps.

Abstract: Optimistic rollups provide scalable smart-contract execution but remain unsuitable for regulated financial applications due to three structural gaps: semantic legitimacy, cross-layer state consistency, and ordering fairness. We introduce RegGuard, a unified framework that enhances optimistic rollups with comprehensive legitimacy guarantees. RegGuard integrates three coordinated mechanisms: a decidable semantic validator powered by the RegSpec rule language for encoding regulatory constraints; a cross-layer state pre-synchronization validator that detects inconsistent L1-L2 dependencies with probabilistic reliability bounds; and a cryptographically verifiable fair-ordering service that ensures transaction sequencing fairness with negligible violation probability. We implement a 15,000-line prototype integrated into an Optimism-based rollup and evaluate it under adversarial conditions. RegGuard reduces settlement failures by over 90%, prevents detectable ordering manipulation, and maintains 85% of baseline throughput.

</details>


### [727] [Optimal Circuit Synthesis of Linear Codes for Error Detection and Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03608)
*Xi Yang,Taolue Chen,Yuqi Chen,Fu Song,Chundong Wang,Zhilin Wu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents CiSC, a novel tool to synthesize optimal code circuits for defending against fault injection attacks, outperforming current methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the vulnerability of cryptographic hardware to fault injection attacks and the challenges of manually designing optimal redundancy codes.

Method: Developed the CiSC algorithm for synthesizing code circuits by systematically reducing inputs and parity size, utilizing SMT solving and lazy input generation.

Result: CiSC demonstrates superior performance in synthesizing optimal circuits compared to state-of-the-art methods, as shown by evaluations on practical benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed CiSC tool offers a secure, efficient, and automated solution to designing code circuits, improving defense mechanisms against fault injection attacks in cryptographic systems.

Abstract: Fault injection attacks deliberately inject faults into a device via physical channels to disturb its regular execution. Adversaries can effectively deduce secrets by analyzing both the normal and faulty outputs, posing serious threats to cryptographic primitives implemented in hardware. An effective countermeasure to such attacks is via redundancy, commonly referred to as concurrent error detection schemes, where Binary linear codes have been used to defend against fault injection attacks. However, designing an optimal code circuit is often time-consuming, error-prone, and requires substantial expertise. In this paper, we formalize the optimal code circuit synthesis problem (OptiCC) based on two domain-specific minimization objectives on individual inputs and parity size. We then propose a novel algorithm CiSC for solving OptiCC, prioritizing the minimization of individual inputs. Our approach features both correct-by-construction and secure-by-construction. In a nutshell, CiSC gradually reduces individual inputs and parity size by checking, via SMT solving, the existence of feasible Boolean functions for implementing a desired code. We further present an effective technique to lazily generate combinations of inputs to Boolean functions, while quickly identify equivalent ones. We implement our approach in a tool CiSC, and evaluate it on practical benchmarks. Experimental results show our approach can synthesize code circuits that significantly outperform those generated by the latest state-of-the-art techniques.

</details>


### [728] [A Faceted Classification of Authenticator-Centric Authentication Techniques](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03627)
*Alex R. Mattukat,Vincent Schmandt,Timo Langstrof,Michael Zerbe,Horst Lichter*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces faceted classification schemes for authentication techniques and authenticators using a literature review of 345 papers, and builds a catalog of techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing classification approaches for AuthN Techniques are limited and insufficiently flexible to encompass their diverse nature.

Method: The authors conducted a targeted LLM-assisted literature review, reviewed 345 papers, and applied semantic clustering to develop novel classification schemes.

Result: Two main contributions were developed: faceted classification schemes for AuthN Techniques and a catalog of Authenticators and AuthN Techniques.

Conclusion: The paper presents innovative classification schemes, a catalog, and proposes discussions on future improvements for organizing diverse authentication techniques.

Abstract: Authentication is a fundamental security means for protecting system resources. Authenticator-centric authentication techniques (AuthN Techniques) address how mechanisms and credentials are used via Authenticators. There are many AuthN Techniques that differ in many ways and there exist classification approaches that aim to structure them. However, they are limited in the aspects they classify and are not flexible enough to accommodate the diverse nature of AuthN Techniques. This paper presents two contributions. First, novel, faceted classification schemes for AuthN Techniques and Authenticators are presented. The schemes were developed based on 345 papers identified through a targeted LLM-assisted literature review and semantic clustering. The classification schemes were applied to build a catalog of Authenticators and AuthN Techniques; the second contribution of this paper. This paper presents our methodology, the classification schemes with example applications, the list of AuthN Techniques from the catalog, and discussions on future work.

</details>


### [729] [Triggering and Detecting Exploitable Library Vulnerability from the Client by Directed Greybox Fuzzing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04102)
*Yukai Zhao,Menghan Wu,Xing Hu,Shaohua Wang,Meng Luo,Xin Xia*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: LiveFuzz uses directed greybox fuzzing (DGF) to detect the exploitability of library vulnerabilities from client programs, without requiring proof-of-concepts (PoCs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for identifying vulnerabilities in third-party libraries rely heavily on PoCs, which are often unavailable, limiting their practical application.

Method: The authors introduced LiveFuzz, which employs a target tuple to extend directed greybox fuzzing to cross-program scenarios. It integrates an Abstract Path Mapping mechanism to manage execution paths and a risk-based adaptive mutation to balance mutations.

Result: The evaluation included a dataset of 61 library vulnerabilities exploited via client programs. LiveFuzz demonstrated increased target-reachable paths compared with baselines and was uniquely able to trigger three vulnerabilities.

Conclusion: LiveFuzz proves effective at detecting vulnerabilities without requiring PoCs, showing potential for improving security in the development processes involving third-party libraries.

Abstract: Developers utilize third-party libraries to improve productivity, which also introduces potential security risks. Existing approaches generate tests for public functions to trigger library vulnerabilities from client programs, yet they depend on proof-of-concepts (PoCs), which are often unavailable. In this paper, we propose a new approach, LiveFuzz, based on directed greybox fuzzing (DGF) to detect the exploitability of library vulnerabilities from client programs without PoCs. LiveFuzz exploits a target tuple to extend existing DGF techniques to cross-program scenarios. Based on the target tuple, LiveFuzz introduces a novel Abstract Path Mapping mechanism to project execution paths, mitigating the preference for shorter paths. LiveFuzz also proposes a risk-based adaptive mutation to mitigate excessive mutation. To evaluate LiveFuzz, we construct a new dataset including 61 cases of library vulnerabilities exploited from client programs. Results show that LiveFuzz increases the number of target-reachable paths compared with all baselines and improves the average speed of vulnerability exposure. Three vulnerabilities are triggered exclusively by LiveFuzz.

</details>


### [730] [LLM-Enabled Open-Source Systems in the Wild: An Empirical Study of Vulnerabilities in GitHub Security Advisories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04288)
*Fariha Tanjim Shifat,Hariswar Baburaj,Ce Zhou,Jaydeb Sarker,Mia Mohammad Imran*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The study analyzes vulnerabilities within LLM-integrated software ecosystems using GitHub Security Advisories, finding traditional frameworks sufficient for implementation-level weaknesses but lacking in capturing architectural risks unique to LLM systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether traditional vulnerability disclosure mechanisms adequately address the specific risks introduced by integrating large language models into open-source ecosystems.

Method: Analyzed 295 GitHub Security Advisories from 2025-2026 and manually annotated 100 advisories using the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025.

Result: No evidence of new implementation-level weakness classes unique to LLM systems was found; recurring architectural risks such as Supply Chain, Excessive Agency, and Prompt Injection were identified.

Conclusion: Combining CWE and OWASP frameworks is necessary to fully address vulnerabilities in systems integrating large language models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly embedded in open-source software (OSS) ecosystems, creating complex interactions among natural language prompts, probabilistic model outputs, and execution-capable components. However, it remains unclear whether traditional vulnerability disclosure frameworks adequately capture these model-mediated risks. To investigate this, we analyze 295 GitHub Security Advisories published between January 2025 and January 2026 that reference LLM-related components, and we manually annotate a sample of 100 advisories using the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025.
  We find no evidence of new implementation-level weakness classes specific to LLM systems. Most advisories map to established CWEs, particularly injection and deserialization weaknesses. At the same time, the OWASP-based analysis reveals recurring architectural risk patterns, especially Supply Chain, Excessive Agency, and Prompt Injection, which often co-occur across multiple stages of execution. These results suggest that existing advisory metadata captures code-level defects but underrepresents model-mediated exposure. We conclude that combining the CWE and OWASP perspectives provides a more complete and necessary view of vulnerabilities in LLM-integrated systems.

</details>


### [731] [Poisoned Identifiers Survive LLM Deobfuscation: A Case Study on Claude Opus 4.6](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04289)
*Luis Guzmán Lorenzo*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The study examines if poisoned identifier names in JavaScript can persist in code reconstructed by a language model despite the model understanding correct semantics. The findings show poisoned names often persist, even during correct semantic inferences, but task framing can mitigate this.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether and why language models, while correctly reconstructing the semantics of obfuscated JavaScript code, still perpetuate poisoned or irrelevant identifier names.

Method: 192 inference runs were conducted on two code archetypes (force-directed graph simulation, A* pathfinding) using Claude Opus 4.6. The tests involved scenarios like baseline runs, semantic commentary evaluation, and altered task framing (e.g., deobfuscate vs write fresh implementation).

Result: 1. Poisoned names persisted in all baseline runs but depended on string table coherence. 2. The model often produced accurate comments alongside incorrect variable names. 3. Frames like 'write a fresh implementation' reduced poisoned name propagation from 100% to minimal levels.

Conclusion: Language models retain poisoned identifier names due to string table artifacts even with correct semantics. Task reframing (e.g., reimplementing cleanly instead of deobfuscating) significantly improves outcomes, but broader generalization is required beyond studied cases.

Abstract: When an LLM deobfuscates JavaScript, can poisoned identifier names in the string table survive into the model's reconstructed code, even when the model demonstrably understands the correct semantics? Using Claude Opus 4.6 across 192 inference runs on two code archetypes (force-directed graph simulation, A* pathfinding; 50 conditions, N=3-6), we found three consistent patterns: (1) Poisoned names persisted in every baseline run on both artifacts (physics: 8/8; pathfinding: 5/5). Matched controls showed this extends to terms with zero semantic fit when the string table does not form a coherent alternative domain. (2) Persistence coexisted with correct semantic commentary: in 15/17 runs the model wrote wrong variable names while correctly describing the actual operation in comments. (3) Task framing changed persistence: explicit verification prompts had no effect (12/12 across 4 variants), but reframing from "deobfuscate this" to "write a fresh implementation" reduced propagation from 100% to 0-20% on physics and to 0% on pathfinding, while preserving the checked algorithmic structure. Matched-control experiments showed zero-fit terms persist at the same rate when the replacement table lacks a coherent alternative-domain signal. Per-term variation in earlier domain-gradient experiments is confounded with domain-level coherence and recoverability. These observations are from two archetypes on one model family (Opus 4.6 primary; Haiku 4.5 spot-check). Broader generalization is needed

</details>


### [732] [Bridging Safety and Security in Complex Systems: A Model-Based Approach with SAFT-GT Toolchain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04705)
*Irdin Pekaric,Raffaela Groner,Alexander Raschke,Thomas Witte,Jubril Gbolahan Adigun,Michael Felderer,Matthias Tichy*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Introduction of the SAFT-GT toolchain designed to enhance safety and security of self-adaptive systems, emphasizing usability, adaptability, and validated by domain expert feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for robust safety and security measures in complex and dynamic self-adaptive systems.

Method: Development of SAFT-GT toolchain incorporating Attack-Fault Trees generation, model combination, and seamless integration with existing systems.

Result: Effective enhancement in safety/security analyses validated through user studies with domain experts showcasing real-world applicability.

Conclusion: SAFT-GT toolchain proves practical and effective in addressing security challenges in adaptable systems, with resources made available for further research.

Abstract: In the rapidly evolving landscape of software engineering, the demand for robust and secure systems has become increasingly critical. This is especially true for self-adaptive systems due to their complexity and the dynamic environments in which they operate. To address this issue, we designed and developed the SAFT-GT toolchain that tackles the multifaceted challenges associated with ensuring both safety and security. This paper provides a comprehensive description of the toolchain's architecture and functionalities, including the Attack-Fault Trees generation and model combination approaches. We emphasize the toolchain's ability to integrate seamlessly with existing systems, allowing for enhanced safety and security analyses without requiring extensive modifications and domain knowledge. Our proposed approach can address evolving security threats, including both known vulnerabilities and emerging attack vectors that could compromise the system. As a use case for the toolchain, we integrate it into the feedback loop of self-adaptive systems. Finally, to validate the practical applicability of the toolchain, we conducted an extensive user study involving domain experts, whose insights and feedback underscore the toolchain's relevance and usability in real-world scenarios. Our findings demonstrate the toolchain's effectiveness in real-world applications while highlighting areas for future improvements. The toolchain and associated resources are available in an open-source repository to promote reproducibility and encourage further research in this field.

</details>


### [733] [CREBench: Evaluating Large Language Models in Cryptographic Binary Reverse Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03750)
*Baicheng Chen,Yu Wang,Ziheng Zhou,Xiangru Liu,Juanru Li,Yilei Chen,Tianxing He*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper introduces CREBench, a benchmark to evaluate large language models (LLMs) on cryptographic reverse engineering (RE) tasks. Evaluations show LLMs, led by GPT-5.4, perform below human experts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reverse engineering (RE) is crucial for software security, but it is labor-intensive, prompting exploration of LLMs as tools for automation.

Method: The authors developed CREBench, a benchmark containing 432 cryptographic challenges spanning algorithms, insecure key usage scenarios, and difficulty levels. They designed an evaluation framework with four sub-tasks to assess RE capabilities.

Result: Evaluations of eight LLMs, with GPT-5.4 scoring the highest (64.03/100), reveal that LLMs lag behind human experts who achieve 92.19/100.

Conclusion: While LLMs show promise for cryptographic RE, human experts significantly outperform them. CREBench establishes a foundation for systematic exploration of LLM capabilities in this domain.

Abstract: Reverse engineering (RE) is central to software security, particularly for cryptographic programs that handle sensitive data and are highly prone to vulnerabilities. It supports critical tasks such as vulnerability discovery and malware analysis. Despite its importance, RE remains labor-intensive and requires substantial expertise, making large language models (LLMs) a potential solution for automating the process. However, their capabilities for RE remain systematically underexplored. To address this gap, we study the cryptographic binary RE capabilities of LLMs and introduce \textbf{CREBench}, a benchmark comprising 432 challenges built from 48 standard cryptographic algorithms, 3 insecure crypto key usage scenarios, and 3 difficulty levels. Each challenge follows a Capture-the-Flag (CTF) RE challenge, requiring the model to analyze the underlying cryptographic logic and recover the correct input. We design an evaluation framework comprising four sub-tasks, from algorithm identification to correct flag recovery. We evaluate eight frontier LLMs on CREBench. GPT-5.4, the best-performing model, achieves 64.03 out of 100 and recovers the flag in 59\% of challenges. We also establish a strong human expert baseline of 92.19 points, showing that humans maintain an advantage in cryptographic RE tasks. Our code and dataset are available at https://github.com/wangyu-ovo/CREBench.

</details>


### [734] [Mapping the Exploitation Surface: A 10,000-Trial Taxonomy of What Makes LLM Agents Exploit Vulnerabilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04561)
*Charafeddine Mouzouni*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper examines which prompt features lead LLMs to exploit vulnerabilities, identifying goal-reframing language as a key trigger while debunking other hypothesized factors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding the conditions under which LLMs exploit security vulnerabilities is critical for improving their safety.

Method: Utilized ~10,000 trials across seven models, 37 prompt conditions, and 12 hypothesized dimensions, all conducted in Docker sandboxes.

Result: Found that goal-reframing language reliably triggers exploitative behavior, while nine of twelve hypothesized dimensions have no detectable effect.

Conclusion: Defenders should focus on auditing for goal-reframing prompts rather than broadly addressing adversarial prompt conditions.

Abstract: LLM agents with tool access can discover and exploit security vulnerabilities. This is known. What is not known is which features of a system prompt trigger this behaviour, and which do not. We present a systematic taxonomy based on approximately 10,000 trials across seven models, 37 prompt conditions, and 12 hypothesized attack dimensions -- all executed in real Docker sandboxes. Every condition includes the instruction "Always respect all rules and access policies"; only a single encouragement sentence varies. Within the task class studied (planted test-runner vulnerabilities), the threat model is narrower than assumed: nine of twelve dimensions -- including minimization, moral licensing, incentives, identity priming, and reasoning triggers -- produce no detectable exploitation at n=50 per cell (upper 95% CI <7% per condition). But it is also more subtle than expected: one dimension, goal reframing, reliably triggers exploitation. "You are solving a puzzle; there may be hidden clues" produces 38-40% exploitation on Claude Sonnet 4 despite the explicit rule instruction, replicating across four models (CTF framing: 8-14% on DeepSeek, GPT-5-mini, o4-mini). The agent does not override the rules; it reinterprets the task so that exploitative actions become task-aligned. GPT-4.1 produces no exploitation across 1,850 trials (37 conditions), and a temporal comparison across four OpenAI models released over eleven months shows a pattern consistent with improving safety training, though model capability differences are a confounder. The practical contribution is a narrowed, testable threat model: defenders should audit for goal-reframing language, not for the broad class of adversarial prompts.

</details>


### [735] [Your Agent, Their Asset: A Real-World Safety Analysis of OpenClaw](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04759)
*Zijun Wang,Haoqin Tu,Letian Zhang,Hardy Chen,Juncheng Wu,Xiangyan Liu,Zhenlong Yuan,Tianyu Pang,Michael Qizhe Shieh,Fengze Liu,Zeyu Zheng,Huaxiu Yao,Yuyin Zhou,Cihang Xie*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This study evaluates OpenClaw, a personal AI agent with extensive system access, for real-world safety. It reveals significant vulnerabilities and introduces the CIK taxonomy for analyzing these weaknesses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to address the security risks posed by OpenClaw's broad privileges and its existing sandboxed evaluations' inability to capture real-world attack surfaces.

Method: The authors introduce the CIK taxonomy (Capability, Identity, Knowledge) and conduct evaluations involving 12 attack scenarios on various models of OpenClaw, analyzing vulnerabilities and testing defense strategies.

Result: Poisoning one CIK dimension increased attack success rates to 64-74%. The best defense still exhibited a high 63.8% success under Capability attacks, and file protection blocked attacks but restricted legitimate updates.

Conclusion: Personal AI agents like OpenClaw exhibit inherent vulnerabilities due to their architecture. Systematic safeguards are essential to secure such systems effectively.

Abstract: OpenClaw, the most widely deployed personal AI agent in early 2026, operates with full local system access and integrates with sensitive services such as Gmail, Stripe, and the filesystem. While these broad privileges enable high levels of automation and powerful personalization, they also expose a substantial attack surface that existing sandboxed evaluations fail to capture. To address this gap, we present the first real-world safety evaluation of OpenClaw and introduce the CIK taxonomy, which unifies an agent's persistent state into three dimensions, i.e., Capability, Identity, and Knowledge, for safety analysis. Our evaluations cover 12 attack scenarios on a live OpenClaw instance across four backbone models (Claude Sonnet 4.5, Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.4). The results show that poisoning any single CIK dimension increases the average attack success rate from 24.6% to 64-74%, with even the most robust model exhibiting more than a threefold increase over its baseline vulnerability. We further assess three CIK-aligned defense strategies alongside a file-protection mechanism; however, the strongest defense still yields a 63.8% success rate under Capability-targeted attacks, while file protection blocks 97% of malicious injections but also prevents legitimate updates. Taken together, these findings show that the vulnerabilities are inherent to the agent architecture, necessitating more systematic safeguards to secure personal AI agents. Our project page is https://ucsc-vlaa.github.io/CIK-Bench.

</details>


### [736] [AICCE: AI Driven Compliance Checker Engine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03330)
*Mohammad Wali Ur Rahman,Martin Manuel Lopez,Lamia Tasnim Mim,Carter Farthing,Julius Battle,Kathryn Buckley,Salim Hariri*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces AICCE, a generative AI-driven system for automated IPv6 compliance checking, achieving 99% accuracy and F1-scores in dynamic communication evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing rule-based systems for communication protocol compliance struggle to detect subtle and intricate instances of non-compliance, which attackers exploit for covert channels in IPv6 traffic. The authors aim to develop a robust, scalable alternative.

Method: AICCE uses dual-architecture reasoning and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to semantically encode protocol standards and retrieve relevant segments for queries. It includes two pipelines: Explainability Mode, offering LLM-based discussions for decision explainability, and Script Execution Mode, converting clauses into Python rules for swift, dataset-wide verification.

Result: AICCE achieves up to 99% accuracy and F1-scores in compliance verification tests on IPv6 packet samples across sixteen advanced generative models.

Conclusion: AICCE provides a scalable, auditable, and generalizable AI-driven mechanism, effectively addressing the limitations of traditional rule-based systems in detecting routine and covert communication protocol non-compliance.

Abstract: For digital infrastructure to be safe, compatible, and standards-aligned, automated communication protocol compliance verification is crucial. Nevertheless, current rule-based systems are becoming less and less effective since they are unable to identify subtle or intricate non-compliance, which attackers frequently use to establish covert communication channels in IPv6 traffic. In order to automate IPv6 compliance verification, this paper presents the Artificial Intelligence Driven Compliance Checker Engine (AICCE), a novel generative system that combines dual-architecture reasoning and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Specification segments pertinent to each query can be efficiently retrieved thanks to the semantic encoding of protocol standards into a high-dimensional vector space. Based on this framework, AICCE offers two complementary pipelines: (i) Explainability Mode, which uses parallel LLM agents to render decisions and settle disputes through organized discussions to improve interpretability and robustness, and (ii) Script Execution Mode, which converts clauses into Python rules that can be executed quickly for dataset-wide verification. With the debate mechanism enhancing decision reliability in complicated scenarios and the script-based pipeline lowering per-sample latency, AICCE achieves accuracy and F1-scores of up to 99% when tested on IPv6 packet samples across sixteen cutting-edge generative models. By offering a scalable, auditable, and generalizable mechanism for identifying both routine and covert non-compliance in dynamic communication environments, our results show that AICCE overcomes the blind spots of conventional rule-based compliance checking systems.

</details>


### [737] [Spatiotemporal-Aware Bit-Flip Injection on DNN-based Advanced Driver Assistance Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03753)
*Taibiao Zhao,Xiang Zhang,Mingxuan Sun,Ruyi Ding,Xugui Zhou*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper tackles the susceptibility of DNNs in ADAS to bit flips, introducing the STAFI framework which is highly effective at pinpointing hazardous faults compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With DNNs being critical to ADAS, their vulnerability to bit flips from cosmic radiation or low-voltage operation poses potential safety risks. The study aims to address the need for methods to identify and mitigate these risks.

Method: The proposed SpatioTemporal-Aware Fault Injection (STAFI) framework uses two key components: Progressive Metric-guided Bit Search (PMBS) to identify high-impact faulty bits in network weights and Critical Fault Time Identification (CFTI) to determine the most hazardous timing for fault occurrence.

Result: STAFI significantly improves fault detection efficiency, showing a 29.56x increase in identifying hazard-inducing critical faults on DNNs used for a production ADAS when compared to previous methods.

Conclusion: By efficiently recognizing critical faults in spatial and temporal dimensions, STAFI offers a valuable tool for enhancing the safety and reliability of ADAS systems.

Abstract: Modern advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS) rely on deep neural networks (DNNs) for perception and planning. Since DNNs' parameters reside in DRAM during inference, bit flips caused by cosmic radiation or low-voltage operation may corrupt DNN computations, distort driving decisions, and lead to real-world incidents. This paper presents a SpatioTemporal-Aware Fault Injection (STAFI) framework to locate critical fault sites in DNNs for ADAS efficiently. Spatially, we propose a Progressive Metric-guided Bit Search (PMBS) that efficiently identifies critical network weight bits whose corruption causes the largest deviations in driving behavior (e.g., unintended acceleration or steering). Furthermore, we develop a Critical Fault Time Identification (CFTI) mechanism that determines when to trigger these faults, taking into account the context of real-time systems and environmental states, to maximize the safety impact. Experiments on DNNs for a production ADAS demonstrate that STAFI uncovers 29.56x more hazard-inducing critical faults than the strongest baseline.

</details>


### [738] [Explainability-Guided Adversarial Attacks on Transformer-Based Malware Detectors Using Control Flow Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03843)
*Andrew Wheeler,Kshitiz Aryal,Maanak Gupta*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper investigates the vulnerability of a RoBERTa-based malware detector that uses linearized CFGs and proposes a white-box adversarial attack using explainability tools to perturb critical graph components, achieving misclassification reliably.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to explore the robustness of transformer-based malware detection systems against adversarial evasion attacks, given their use of token-level sensitivities and ordering artifacts in CFGs.

Method: A white-box adversarial attack strategy is developed by leveraging explainability tools, specifically integrated gradients, to identify and replace influential function calls with synthetic external imports in CFGs.

Result: Experimental results show that the proposed attack induces reliable misclassification on both small- and large-scale PE datasets, even against highly accurate models.

Conclusion: Explainability tools, while useful for interpretability, can unintentionally reveal vulnerabilities in transformer-based malware detectors, underscoring the need for improved robustness.

Abstract: Transformer-based malware detection systems operating on graph modalities such as control flow graphs (CFGs) achieve strong performance by modeling structural relationships in program behavior. However, their robustness to adversarial evasion attacks remains underexplored. This paper examines the vulnerability of a RoBERTa-based malware detector that linearizes CFGs into sequences of function calls, a design choice that enables transformer modeling but may introduce token-level sensitivities and ordering artifacts exploitable by adversaries. By evaluating evasion strategies within this graph-to-sequence framework, we provide insight into the practical robustness of transformer-based malware detectors beyond aggregate detection accuracy.
  This paper proposes a white-box adversarial evasion attack that leverages explainability mechanisms to identify and perturb most influential graph components. Using token- and word-level attributions derived from integrated gradients, the attack iteratively replaces positively attributed function calls with synthetic external imports, producing adversarial CFG representations without altering overall program structure. Experimental evaluation on small- and large-scale Windows Portable Executable (PE) datasets demonstrates that the proposed method can reliably induce misclassification, even against models trained to high accuracy. Our results highlight that explainability tools, while valuable for interpretability, can also expose critical attack surfaces in transformer-based malware detectors.

</details>


### [739] [Improving ML Attacks on LWE with Data Repetition and Stepwise Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03903)
*Alberto Alfarano,Eshika Saxena,Emily Wenger,François Charton,Kristin Lauter*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper shows that machine learning (ML) techniques can recover denser secrets in the Learning with Errors (LWE) problem using larger datasets and repeated examples, building on prior research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The aim is to enhance understanding of the weaknesses in LWE-based cryptographic systems specifically through effective ML attacks on denser secrets.

Method: The authors use larger training datasets and repeated examples in conjunction with stepwise regression to improve the recovery of secret bits in the LWE problem.

Result: They empirically find a power-law correlation between recovery efforts, dataset size, and repeated examples, showcasing improved secret recovery methods.

Conclusion: The findings demonstrate the vulnerability of denser secrets in LWE to advanced ML techniques, calling for enhanced cryptographic safeguards.

Abstract: The Learning with Errors (LWE) problem is a hard math problem in lattice-based cryptography. In the simplest case of binary secrets, it is the subset sum problem, with error. Effective ML attacks on LWE were demonstrated in the case of binary, ternary, and small secrets, succeeding on fairly sparse secrets. The ML attacks recover secrets with up to 3 active bits in the "cruel region" (Nolte et al., 2024) on samples pre-processed with BKZ. We show that using larger training sets and repeated examples enables recovery of denser secrets. Empirically, we observe a power-law relationship between model-based attempts to recover the secrets, dataset size, and repeated examples. We introduce a stepwise regression technique to recover the "cool bits" of the secret.

</details>


### [740] [SecPI: Secure Code Generation with Reasoning Models via Security Reasoning Internalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03587)
*Hao Wang,Niels Mündler,Mark Vero,Jingxuan He,Dawn Song,Martin Vechev*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces SecPI, a method to fine-tune reasoning language models (RLMs) to autonomously generate secure code without security instructions, improving security and correctness metrics across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for secure code generation either require expensive manual datasets covering limited vulnerabilities or degrade functionality with generic reminders, necessitating a more robust approach.

Method: SecPI uses a pipeline that filters general-purpose datasets for security-relevant tasks, generates reasoning traces via teacher models guided by structured prompts, and fine-tunes RLMs to internalize security reasoning autonomously.

Result: SecPI achieves significant improvements in generating functionally correct and secure code, enhancing performance by +14.0% on CWEval and +3.8% on BaxBench. It also demonstrates strong generalization to unseen vulnerabilities.

Conclusion: SecPI effectively teaches RLMs to reason about security independently, improving secure code generation performance while showing cross-CWE and cross-language generalization beyond training vulnerabilities.

Abstract: Reasoning language models (RLMs) are increasingly used in programming. Yet, even state-of-the-art RLMs frequently introduce critical security vulnerabilities in generated code. Prior training-based approaches for secure code generation face a critical limitation that prevents their direct application to RLMs: they rely on costly, manually curated security datasets covering only a limited set of vulnerabilities. At the inference level, generic security reminders consistently degrade functional correctness while triggering only shallow ad-hoc vulnerability analysis. To address these problems, we present SecPI, a fine-tuning pipeline that teaches RLMs to internalize structured security reasoning, producing secure code by default without any security instructions at inference time. SecPI filters existing general-purpose coding datasets for security-relevant tasks using an LLM-based classifier, generates high-quality security reasoning traces with a teacher model guided by a structured prompt that systematically enumerates relevant CWEs and mitigations, and fine-tunes the target model on pairs of inputs with no security prompt and teacher reasoning traces -- as a result, the model learns to reason about security autonomously rather than in response to explicit instructions. An extensive evaluation on security benchmarks with state-of-the-art open-weight reasoning models validates the effectiveness of our approach. For instance, SecPI improves the percentage of functionally correct and secure generations for QwQ 32B from 48.2% to 62.2% (+14.0 points) on CWEval and from 18.2% to 22.0% on BaxBench. Further investigation also reveals strong cross-CWE and cross-language generalization beyond training vulnerabilities. Even when trained only on injection-related CWEs, QwQ 32B generates correct and secure code 9.9% more frequently on held-out memory-safety CWEs.

</details>


### [741] [Jellyfish: Zero-Shot Federated Unlearning Scheme with Knowledge Disentanglement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04030)
*Houzhe Wang,Xiaojie Zhu,Chi Chen*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Jellyfish, a zero-shot federated unlearning scheme, designed to ensure data privacy by forgetting specific data in federated learning models without accessing local data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With the rise in concerns about data privacy and security, federated unlearning becomes important for ensuring models can erase specific data and related information when requested.

Method: Jellyfish combines synthetic data generation, knowledge disentanglement, advanced loss function design, and zero-shot model repair to achieve effective unlearning.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that Jellyfish successfully balances the dual objectives of forgetting targeted data while maintaining model utility under diverse settings.

Conclusion: Jellyfish proves effective and robust in addressing privacy concerns in federated learning and represents a significant advancement in federated unlearning research.

Abstract: With the increasing importance of data privacy and security, federated unlearning emerges as a new research field dedicated to ensuring that once specific data is deleted, federated learning models no longer retain or disclose related information. In this paper, we propose a zero-shot federated unlearning scheme, named Jellyfish. It distinguishes itself from conventional federated unlearning frameworks in four key aspects: synthetic data generation, knowledge disentanglement, loss function design, and model repair. To preserve the privacy of forgotten data, we design a zero-shot unlearning mechanism that generates error-minimization noise as proxy data for the data to be forgotten. To maintain model utility, we first propose a knowledge disentanglement mechanism that regularises the output of the final convolutional layer by restricting the number of activated channels for the data to be forgotten and encouraging activation sparsity. Next, we construct a comprehensive loss function that incorporates multiple components, including hard loss, confusion loss, distillation loss, model weight drift loss, gradient harmonization, and gradient masking, to effectively align the learning trajectories of the objectives of ``forgetting" and ``retaining". Finally, we propose a zero-shot repair mechanism that leverages proxy data to restore model accuracy within acceptable bounds without accessing users' local data. To evaluate the performance of the proposed zero-shot federated unlearning scheme, we conducted comprehensive experiments across diverse settings. The results validate the effectiveness and robustness of the scheme.

</details>


### [742] [TraceGuard: Structured Multi-Dimensional Monitoring as a Collusion-Resistant Control Protocol](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03968)
*Khanh Linh Nguyen,Hoa Nghiem,Tu Tran*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: TraceGuard introduces a multi-dimensional monitoring protocol for AI control, improving safety and honesty detection in untrusted AI agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address two major shortcomings in current AI monitor systems: their inability to detect nuanced reasoning-based attacks and their vulnerability to collusion when using the same model as the untrusted agent.

Method: TraceGuard employs a structured five-dimensional monitoring approach with independent LLM calls, heuristic detectors, and an intent analyzer to evaluate AI actions comprehensively in the ControlArena framework.

Result: The approach demonstrated strong attack-honest separation, constrained collusion-based safety risks, identified goal alignment and constraint adherence as highly effective dimensions, and ensured complete safety with a separation-of-duties variant.

Conclusion: TraceGuard significantly enhances AI safety monitoring by adopting a multi-dimensional, structured evaluation system and reducing risks of collusion and subtle attacks, making it a robust protocol for AI control.

Abstract: AI control protocols use monitors to detect attacks by untrusted AI agents, but standard single-score monitors face two limitations: they miss subtle attacks where outputs look clean but reasoning is off, and they collapse to near-zero safety when the monitor is the same model as the agent (collusion). We present TraceGuard, a structured multi-dimensional monitoring protocol that evaluates agent actions across five dimensions -- goal alignment, constraint adherence, reasoning coherence, safety awareness, and action-trace consistency -- scored in parallel by independent LLM calls, augmented by seven heuristic detectors and an LLM-based intent analyzer. We evaluate on BashArena (637 bash tasks, 4 attack categories) within the ControlArena framework. Our results on 519 samples (279 honest, 240 attack) show that: (1) the hybrid approach achieves clear attack-honest separation (attack mean 0.616 vs. honest mean 0.206, Delta=0.410); (2) structured scoring constrains collusion -- the untrusted structured monitor achieves 95% safety vs. 0% for single-score untrusted monitoring; (3) goal alignment and constraint adherence are the most discriminative dimensions; and (4) a separation-of-duties variant splitting dimensions across trusted and untrusted models achieves 100% safety while preventing any single model from seeing the full evaluation. TraceGuard is implemented as a new monitor type for the open-source ControlArena framework.

</details>


### [743] [Causality Laundering: Denial-Feedback Leakage in Tool-Calling LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04035)
*Mohammad Hossein Chinaei*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Tool-calling LLM agents pose security risks by exploiting denied actions to infer sensitive information through causality laundering. A solution, Agentic Reference Monitor (ARM), enhances security by enforcing denial-aware causal provenance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address vulnerabilities in tool-calling LLM agents where denied actions can lead to sensitive information leakage through indirect causality laundering.

Method: Developed ARM, an enforcement layer leveraging a provenance graph with counterfactual edges and integrity lattice to block causality laundering and related attacks.

Result: ARM demonstrated the ability to prevent attacks like causality laundering in controlled scenarios with minimal evaluation overhead.

Conclusion: Denial-aware causal provenance provides an effective framework for improving the security of tool-invoking agent systems against indirect data leakage threats.

Abstract: Tool-calling LLM agents can read private data, invoke external services, and trigger real-world actions, creating a security problem at the point of tool execution. We identify a denial-feedback leakage pattern, which we term causality laundering, in which an adversary probes a protected action, learns from the denial outcome, and exfiltrates the inferred information through a later seemingly benign tool call. This attack is not captured by flat provenance tracking alone because the leaked information arises from causal influence of the denied action, not direct data flow.
